title: Ensure '3625 (trace flag)' Database Flag for all Cloud SQL  SQL Server Instances Is Set to 'on'

input_row.title: Ensure '3625 (trace flag)' Database Flag for all Cloud SQL  SQL Server Instances Is Set to 'on'
input_row.description: It is recommended to set 3625 (trace flag) database flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to on.
input_row.rationale: Microsoft SQL Trace Flags are frequently used to diagnose performance issues or to debug stored procedures or complex computer systems, but they may also be recommended by Microsoft Support to address behavior that is negatively impacting a specific workload. All documented trace flags and those recommended by Microsoft Support are fully supported in a production environment when used as directed. 3625(trace log) Limits the amount of information returned to users who are not members of the sysadmin fixed server role, by masking the parameters of some error messages using '******'. Setting this in a Google Cloud flag for the instance allows for security through obscurity and prevents the disclosure of sensitive information, hence this is recommended to set this flag globally to on to prevent the flag having been left off, or changed by bad actors. This recommendation is applicable to SQL Server database instances.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Instance Overview . Ensure the database flag 3625 that has been set is listed under the Database flags section. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Ensure the below command returns on for every Cloud SQL SQL Server database instance gcloud sql instances describe --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="3625")|.value'
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the SQL Server instance for which you want to enable to database flag. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down to the Flags section. 5. To set a flag that has not been set on the instance before, click Add item, choose the flag 3625 from the drop-down menu, and set its value to on. 6. Click Save to save your changes. 7. Confirm your changes under Flags on the Overview page. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Configure the 3625 database flag for every Cloud SQL SQL Server database instance using the below command. gcloud sql instances patch --database-flags "3625=on" Note: This command will overwrite all database flags previously set. To keep those and add new ones, include the values for all flags you want set on the instance; any flag not specifically included is set to its default value. For flags that do not take a value, specify the flag name followed by an equals sign ("=").
input_row.impact: Changing flags on a database may cause it to be restarted. The best time to do this is at a time where there is low usage.
input_row.default_value: MS SQL Server implementations by default have trace flags, including the '3625' flag, turned off, as they are used for logging purposes.
input_row.additional_information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/sqlserver/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note: some database flag settings can affect instance availability or stability, and remove the instance from the Cloud SQL SLA. For information about these flags, see Operational Guidelines. Note: Configuring the above flag restarts the Cloud SQL instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- All criteria point towards an automated approach:
  - The audit field includes a command using `gcloud` to verify the database flag setting.
  - GCP APIs can query this property through the specified commands.
  - This involves a standard GCP service property (database flags for Cloud SQL).
  - Compliance can be verified using the Google Cloud SDK.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_instance_database_flag_3625_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`) to verify the database flag configuration:
  - Command to retrieve the values of database flags for SQL Server instances:
    ```bash
    gcloud sql instances describe <INSTANCE_NAME> --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="3625") | .value'
    ```
- The resource to enumerate will be all Cloud SQL SQL Server instances managed under the project.
- The expected configuration property to validate is the flag `3625` with a value of `on`.
- Pass/Fail criteria:
  - **Pass:** If the command returns a value of `on` for the flag `3625`.
  - **Fail:** If the command returns an empty value or any value other than `on`. 

This structure ensures a clear understanding of the compliance control being checked and leverages automation capabilities within GCP.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The use of the `gcloud` command to check the database flag setting is appropriate and leverages GCP's capabilities effectively. The Google Cloud SDK provides the necessary tools to automate this check, making manual verification unnecessary.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9
- The automation details are technically accurate. The `gcloud sql instances describe` command is valid and correctly formatted to retrieve the database flag settings for SQL Server instances. The use of `jq` to filter and select the specific flag is appropriate.
- The program name follows a reasonable convention but could be slightly more descriptive.
- The command correctly identifies the presence and value of the flag `3625`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- Consider enhancing the program name to be more descriptive: `gcp_sql_server_instance_trace_flag_3625_enabled`.
- Ensure that the command accounts for potential variations in output, such as handling instances where the flag might not be set at all. This could be done by adding error handling or additional checks in the script.
- Document any prerequisites for using `jq`, as it may not be installed by default on all systems.
- Consider using `gcloud sql instances list` to dynamically retrieve all instance names if the script is intended to run across multiple instances automatically.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-founded and uses the appropriate GCP tools and commands. The automation approach is sound, and the technical implementation is robust.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Enhance the program name for clarity and consider additional error handling in the script.
- The assessment is otherwise accurate and should be retained with the suggested improvements.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`). The `gcloud sql instances describe` command allows for the retrieval of database flag settings, and the use of `jq` enables filtering for the specific flag `3625`. This approach leverages GCP's capabilities to automate the compliance check effectively, eliminating the need for manual verification.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_sql_server_instance_trace_flag_3625_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Cloud SQL SQL Server Instances
- API/CLI Method: Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud sql instances describe`)
- Properties to Check: `.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="3625") | .value`
- Pass Criteria: The command returns a value of `on` for the flag `3625`.
- Fail Criteria: The command returns an empty value or any value other than `on`.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported the automated approach. The second opinion suggested a slight improvement in the program name for clarity, which has been adopted.
